_comment: Per-moon MEASURED 'feature' facts for the major-moons HUD, each fact individually source-tagged. Every value is a measured or well-established observed fact from a cited mission/instrument or peer-reviewed source -- nothing is a forecast and nothing is invented. Genuinely debated items (Europa/Callisto plumes/ocean specifics, Mimas' young ocean) carry status:'debated'/'possible'/'recent'. Per the phase honesty bar: MOST of these moons have NO weather; the honest substance is orbital mechanics + measured phenomena + real textures. TITAN is the sole exception with a genuine (methane) weather cycle -> weather:true. See docs/MOONS_PHYSICS.md and docs/MOONS_DATA_SOURCES.md.

meta.honesty: measured/observed facts only; debated items flagged; no forecasts; no invented weather (Titan is the only weather moon).

Io.headline: Most volcanically active body in the solar system

activeVolcanoes.source: NASA/JPL; Galileo/Voyager/Juno + ground-based monitoring. ~400 volcanic centres estimated, of which >150 have been observed actively erupting.

tidalHeating.source: Veeder et al. 2012, Icarus (global heat flow ~10^14 W, ~2 W/m^2 average, ~40x Earth's). Powered by tidal heating from the 1:2:4 Laplace resonance.

plumeHeightsKm.source: Galileo / New Horizons imaging: Pele plume ~390 km, Tvashtar ~385 km high (+/- ~30 km). Fine sulfur/SO2 plumes reach hundreds of km above the surface.

hotspotTempsK.value: up to ~1300-1600 K at active silicate lava vents
hotspotTempsK.source: Galileo NIMS / Juno JIRAM: high-temperature silicate volcanism; vent temperatures far above the ~110 K background surface.

Europa.headline: Young ice shell over a subsurface salt-water ocean

subsurfaceOcean.source: Kivelson et al. 2000, Science (Galileo magnetometer: Jupiter-induced magnetic field requires a near-surface conducting layer = salty liquid ocean). NASA: the ocean may hold ~2x the water of Earth's oceans.

youngSurface.source: Galileo imaging (crater counts). One of the youngest, smoothest solid surfaces in the solar system -> active resurfacing.

chaosTerrain.source: Galileo imaging: 'chaos' regions of broken, rotated ice blocks -- evidence of a mobile/observably active ice shell above liquid or warm ice.

plumes.source: Roth et al. 2014 and Sparks et al. 2016-2017 (HST) reported possible water-vapour plumes; NOT confirmed/repeatable -- explicitly DEBATED. Europa Clipper (launched 2024-10-14) is designed to settle it.

Ganymede.headline: Largest moon in the solar system; the only one with its own magnetic field

largestMoon.source: NASA/JPL: Ganymede (r 2631 km) is the largest moon in the solar system -- larger than the planet Mercury (r 2440 km) and Pluto.

intrinsicMagneticField.source: Kivelson et al. 1996, Nature (Galileo): Ganymede is the ONLY moon known to generate its own intrinsic magnetic field (an internal dynamo), producing polar auroras.

subsurfaceOcean.source: Saur et al. 2015, JGR (HST auroral-oscillation constraint) + Galileo data: strong evidence for a subsurface salt-water ocean.

Callisto.headline: Among the oldest, most heavily cratered surfaces in the solar system

mostHeavilyCratered.source: NASA/JPL: Callisto is the most heavily cratered object in the solar system -- an ancient, geologically dead surface saturated with impacts.

possibleOcean.source: Zimmer et al. 2000, Icarus (Galileo magnetometer): an induced magnetic field hints at a deep briny layer. Deep and unconfirmed -- flagged as possible.

Titan.headline: Thick N2 atmosphere with an active methane cycle -- the one moon with real weather

atmospherePressureBar.source: Huygens HASI, Fulchignoni et al. 2005, Nature (surface pressure 146.7 kPa = 1.45 atm -- ~50% denser than Earth's at the surface).

atmosphereN2Percent.source: Cassini/Huygens: atmosphere ~98.4% nitrogen (N2), ~1.4% methane (CH4) + trace hydrocarbons.

surfaceTempK.source: Huygens HASI, Fulchignoni et al. 2005 (surface 93.7 K, -179.5 C).

methaneCycle.source: Cassini/Huygens: an active hydrologic cycle in methane/ethane -- clouds, rain, rivers, lakes and seas. The only world besides Earth with standing surface liquid and genuine weather.

seas.source: Cassini RADAR (north polar seas of liquid methane/ethane): Kraken Mare (largest), Ligeia Mare (2nd), Punga Mare (3rd); together ~80% of Titan's sea+lake area (~691,000 km^2).

Enceladus.headline: South-polar water plumes feeding Saturn's E ring, from a global subsurface ocean

plumes.source: Porco et al. 2006, Science (Cassini): 100+ discrete jets of water vapour + ice grains erupt from the four south-polar 'tiger stripe' fractures (Alexandria, Cairo, Baghdad, Damascus sulci).

feedsSaturnERing.source: Spahn et al. 2006 / Kempf et al. (Cassini CDA): the plume is the dominant source of Saturn's diffuse E ring; Enceladus orbits within it.

subsurfaceOcean.source: Cassini gravity + libration (Iess et al. 2014; Thomas et al. 2016): a GLOBAL subsurface salt-water ocean; plume grains contain salts + silica (hydrothermal chemistry).

geometricAlbedo.source: Verbiscer et al. 2007, Science (geometric albedo 1.375 at 550 nm -- the brightest body in the solar system; fresh-ice plume fallout).

Mimas.headline: The 'Death Star' moon: dominated by the giant Herschel crater

herschelCrater.source: NASA/JPL (Cassini/Voyager): the impact crater Herschel is 139 km across (~1/3 of Mimas' diameter), with a ~6 km central peak -- the 'Death Star' look.

possibleYoungOcean.source: Lainey et al. 2024, Nature: orbital-libration analysis suggests a geologically young subsurface ocean. RECENT result -- flagged as debated.

Iapetus.headline: Two-tone moon: one coal-dark hemisphere, one bright, plus a giant equatorial ridge

twoToneAlbedo.source: NASA/Cassini: the leading hemisphere (Cassini Regio) is coal-dark (geometric albedo 0.03-0.05) while the trailing hemisphere is bright (0.5-0.6) -- a thermal-runaway ice-migration dichotomy.

equatorialRidge.value: ~1300 km long, up to ~20 km high
equatorialRidge.source: Porco et al. 2005 (Cassini) / Ip 2006: a narrow ridge runs along much of Iapetus' equator, giving it a walnut shape.

Triton.headline: Retrograde captured world with active nitrogen geysers -- among the coldest measured

retrogradeOrbit.source: JPL SSD orbital elements (i = 157.3 deg > 90 = retrograde). NASA: Triton almost certainly a captured Kuiper-belt object.

geysers.source: Smith et al. 1989 / Soderblom et al. 1990 (Voyager 2, 1989): >=4 active geysers venting nitrogen gas + dark dust ~8 km high, then drifting >100 km downwind.

thinN2Atmosphere.source: Broadfoot et al. 1989 (Voyager 2): thin nitrogen atmosphere, surface pressure ~1.4 Pa (~14 microbar), ~1/70000 of Earth's.

surfaceTempK.source: Voyager 2 (1989), Tyler et al. 1989 / Broadfoot et al. 1989: ~38 K (-235 C) -- among the coldest surfaces measured in the solar system.

cantaloupeTerrain.source: Voyager 2 imaging (Soderblom et al. 1990): dimpled 'cantaloupe' terrain, unique in the solar system, likely from icy diapirism.
